meta.title: Displacement Governance Diagnostic
meta.editorialNotes: Primary analytical coding completed for all 40 cases. Continuity ratings and political-economy fields are expert analytical judgments and should remain visibly distinguished from documented formal design.

primaryFaultLines.label: Categorical
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when a person is reclassified across legal, administrative or programme categories.

primaryFaultLines.label: Temporal
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when an emergency, project, programme, mandate or funding period expires before displacement-related vulnerability resolves.

primaryFaultLines.label: Territorial
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when a person crosses a border, district, municipality, provider catchment or other jurisdictional boundary.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Systems integration
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Embedding displaced or mobile populations in ordinary public systems, service pathways, registries and administrative procedures.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Fiscal integration
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Embedding displacement-related obligations in durable, recurrent or transferable public financing arrangements.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Sustained institutional responsibility
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Maintaining clear responsibility, authority and accountability across institutions, jurisdictions and displacement phases.

cases.title: AALCO Bangkok Principles
cases.institutionsText: Regional legal framing rests with AALCO member states, but implementation depends on national ministries, immigration authorities, asylum or refugee bodies where they exist, courts, public service agencies, and cooperation with UNHCR or humanitarian actors.

transition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: People who fit refugee or asylum-related categories as understood through regional and international protection norms may be recognised analytically by the instrument. The framework is most relevant to cross-border displacement involving persecution, conflict, or serious protection risk.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee recognition is acknowledged normatively but remains subject to domestic state discretion.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional principles do not assign operational authority to a domestic delivery institution.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No automatic fiscal obligation or financing channel is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Regional principles create a shared protection vocabulary while preserving state discretion over recognition, services, and fiscal responsibility.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People displaced by disasters, development projects, climate stress, statelessness, mixed migration, internal movement, or administrative exclusion are not automatically covered unless national systems or other legal regimes separately recognise them.

cases.finding: The Bangkok Principles create a regional vocabulary for refugee protection, but they do not by themselves carry displaced people into national registration, financing, or service systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The principles provide a sovereignty-preserving normative framework. They allow states to discuss refugee protection in regional terms without requiring accession to the 1951 Refugee Convention or creating automatic domestic entitlements. Their governance function is therefore classificatory and diplomatic rather than administrative or fiscal.
cases.included: People who fit refugee or asylum-related categories as understood through regional and international protection norms may be recognised analytically by the instrument. The framework is most relevant to cross-border displacement involving persecution, conflict, or serious protection risk.
cases.excluded: People displaced by disasters, development projects, climate stress, statelessness, mixed migration, internal movement, or administrative exclusion are not automatically covered unless national systems or other legal regimes separately recognise them.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.
sources: AALCO final text of the Bangkok Principles
Refworld entry on the Bangkok Principles

cases.title: Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement
cases.institutionsText: Primary responsibility rests with national authorities. International humanitarian and development actors support implementation but do not replace sovereign responsibility.
populationGroups: People at risk of internal displacement

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.
mechanisms.label: Statutory mandate assignment

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: IDP membership is defined broadly in normative terms but depends on domestic recognition and implementation.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: National responsibility is affirmed, but operational mandates must be created domestically.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No budget line or financing mechanism follows from the principles themselves.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The principles define national responsibility for IDPs but do not themselves create budgets, delivery systems, registries, or enforceable service entitlements.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People who cross an international border become subject to other legal and institutional regimes. People affected by slower-onset development, climate, livelihood, or administrative displacement may be left out when national systems interpret internal displacement narrowly.

cases.finding: The Guiding Principles define IDP rights across displacement phases, but protection continuity depends on whether states translate them into budgets, mandates, data systems, and ordinary services.
cases.governanceFunction: The principles convert internal displacement into a matter of national responsibility, supported by international law by analogy and human rights standards. Their function is to clarify what protection should cover, while leaving implementation to domestic governance systems and international support arrangements.
cases.included: People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.
cases.excluded: People who cross an international border become subject to other legal and institutional regimes. People affected by slower-onset development, climate, livelihood, or administrative displacement may be left out when national systems interpret internal displacement narrowly.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.
sources: OCHA Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement

cases.title: UN Secretary-General Action Agenda on Internal Displacement
cases.institutionsText: National governments retain primary responsibility. The UN system, development actors, humanitarian agencies, financial institutions, and local authorities are expected to align behind nationally owned prevention and solutions efforts.

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

mechanisms.scope: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
mechanisms.label: Lead-agency responsibility

mechanisms.scope: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
mechanisms.label: Externally financed system expansion

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: IDPs and host communities are framed as development and solutions constituencies rather than only humanitarian caseloads.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The agenda promotes national ownership and UN-system alignment but lacks direct implementation authority.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: It seeks development and solutions finance but does not itself create recurrent national expenditure obligations.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The agenda seeks to move internal displacement from humanitarian management into national planning, development finance, prevention, and solutions systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside if national plans do not recognise them, if displacement data are weak, if solutions are defined narrowly as return, or if financing remains humanitarian and short-term.

cases.finding: The Action Agenda shifts internal displacement from humanitarian management toward development, prevention, and solutions, but success depends on whether states and partners embed IDP inclusion in ordinary planning and financing systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The Action Agenda reframes internal displacement as a cross-cutting governance and development issue rather than a residual humanitarian caseload. It seeks to align national responsibility, UN system support, development financing, prevention, and solutions planning.
cases.included: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
cases.excluded: People may remain outside if national plans do not recognise them, if displacement data are weak, if solutions are defined narrowly as return, or if financing remains humanitarian and short-term.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.
sources: UN Action Agenda on Internal Displacement page

cases.title: Global Compact on Refugees
cases.institutionsText: Host states retain responsibility for domestic systems and legal access. Donors, development banks, UNHCR, UN agencies, NGOs, private actors, and refugees themselves are expected to contribute through the compact's responsibility-sharing architecture.

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.
mechanisms.label: Externally financed system expansion

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee and host-community inclusion remains mediated by host-state policy and refugee status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The compact coordinates voluntary responsibility sharing without transferring sovereign implementation authority.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: External support can enable system expansion but remains pledge- and programme-dependent.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugee inclusion becomes more feasible where host-state policy, international financing, donor commitments, and development cooperation align.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: IDPs, migrants, disaster-displaced people, and development-displaced populations are outside the compact's central refugee frame unless addressed through related national or international mechanisms.

cases.finding: The Global Compact on Refugees promotes inclusion in national systems and more predictable responsibility sharing, but implementation depends on host-state choices, donor financing, and development-system alignment.
cases.governanceFunction: The compact links refugee protection to development cooperation, national systems, host community support, financing, and multi-stakeholder pledging. Its function is to shift refugee response away from purely parallel humanitarian assistance toward more predictable inclusion and burden sharing.
cases.included: Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.
cases.excluded: IDPs, migrants, disaster-displaced people, and development-displaced populations are outside the compact's central refugee frame unless addressed through related national or international mechanisms.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.
sources: Global Compact on Refugees official platform
UNHCR Global Compact on Refugees booklet

cases.title: Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration
cases.institutionsText: States carry primary implementation responsibility. UN Network on Migration, IOM, OHCHR, ILO, UNHCR where relevant, civil society, employers, local governments, and diaspora actors support implementation.
populationGroups: Families and communities affected by migration

transition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.
mechanisms.label: Portable entitlement

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Migrants are recognized through a cooperative framework while admission and status remain sovereign.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Implementation is dispersed across domestic migration, labour and service institutions.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No automatic financing obligation is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The compact promotes rights-based migration cooperation while preserving state control over admission, status, and national implementation.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Refugees remain governed by separate refugee protection frameworks. IDPs and people displaced internally by conflict, disasters, development projects, or climate stress are not the compact's primary population unless migration pathways or cross-border mobility are implicated.

cases.finding: The Global Compact for Migration promotes rights-based and cooperative migration governance, but it separates migrants from refugees and leaves access to services, status, and protection largely dependent on domestic systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The compact organises migration as a whole-of-government and whole-of-society governance issue. It creates a policy vocabulary for cooperation while preserving state sovereignty over admission, status, and national implementation.
cases.included: Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.
cases.excluded: Refugees remain governed by separate refugee protection frameworks. IDPs and people displaced internally by conflict, disasters, development projects, or climate stress are not the compact's primary population unless migration pathways or cross-border mobility are implicated.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.
sources: OHCHR page on the Global Compact for Migration
IOM Global Compact for Migration page
Global Compact for Migration final draft PDF

cases.title: World Bank ESS5
cases.institutionsText: Borrowers implement project obligations under World Bank requirements, with Bank oversight through preparation, appraisal, supervision, environmental and social commitments, and implementation support.

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

interfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.
mechanisms.label: Project-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.
mechanisms.label: Grievance and review

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is bounded to project-affected-person classifications and cut-off rules.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Borrower and project responsibilities are strong during the project but weaken at closure unless transferred.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Costs are project-bounded and designed to close rather than become recurrent public liabilities.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displacement is governed as a project-related environmental and social risk, with obligations bounded by project scope, financing, and supervision.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected indirectly, downstream, cumulatively, across borders, after project closure, or outside the financed project's defined area may be harder to capture unless the project assessment includes them.

cases.finding: ESS5 governs displacement created by World Bank-financed projects, but its protections can end at the project boundary before livelihood, service, and territorial continuity are restored.
cases.governanceFunction: ESS5 translates development finance into procedural and substantive obligations for resettlement, compensation, livelihood restoration, consultation, grievance handling, and mitigation of project-induced displacement.
cases.included: People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.
cases.excluded: People affected indirectly, downstream, cumulatively, across borders, after project closure, or outside the financed project's defined area may be harder to capture unless the project assessment includes them.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.
sources: World Bank Environmental and Social Standards
World Bank Environmental and Social Framework PDF

cases.title: IFC Performance Standard 5
cases.institutionsText: The IFC client carries implementation responsibility, with IFC review and supervision. Government authorities may also be involved in land acquisition, permits, compensation, and local service systems.
populationGroups: People physically displaced by private-sector projects
People economically displaced by private-sector projects

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

interfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.
mechanisms.label: Project-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.
mechanisms.label: Grievance and review

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is tied to private-project impact and client assessment.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Responsibility rests with the client under lender supervision rather than ordinary public systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Mitigation costs are internalized within the project and investment lifecycle.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Private-sector displacement risk is internalized through client obligations, lender requirements, mitigation plans, and project accountability systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People indirectly affected through cumulative impacts, market shifts, downstream effects, informal tenure not recognised in practice, or impacts beyond the project area may be excluded if the client's assessment does not capture them.

cases.finding: IFC Performance Standard 5 extends displacement governance into private-sector finance, but responsibility remains anchored to client risk management and project boundaries rather than public-system inclusion.
cases.governanceFunction: The standard turns displacement risk into a condition of private-sector finance. It requires clients to avoid or minimize displacement, compensate affected people, restore livelihoods, engage communities, and establish grievance mechanisms.
cases.included: People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.
cases.excluded: People indirectly affected through cumulative impacts, market shifts, downstream effects, informal tenure not recognised in practice, or impacts beyond the project area may be excluded if the client's assessment does not capture them.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.

cases.title: Thailand Migrant Health Insurance Scheme
cases.institutionsText: The Ministry of Public Health and linked health service administrators carry core system responsibility, while labour, interior, immigration, and employer-linked processes shape documentation and eligibility pathways.

transition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

interfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

mechanisms.scope: Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Contributory or premium-based inclusion

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Non-citizens can enter health coverage through bounded administrative categories without broader legal membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The health ministry can act where public-health and hospital-financing interests align, while migration authorities retain status control.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Premium and cost-recovery design limits open-ended state exposure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Non-citizens are absorbed into health financing where public health, hospital financing, and migration-management incentives align, without broader legal membership.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without documents, people outside registration windows, refugees outside the relevant administrative channel, people unable to pay premiums, mobile workers, and people whose employer or status changes may remain outside or experience interrupted access.

cases.finding: Thailand's migrant health insurance architecture shows how a public system can absorb non-citizens for health purposes while leaving legal status, portability, and broader social protection unresolved.
cases.governanceFunction: The scheme converts a migration and public health management problem into a bounded health-financing mechanism. It reduces uncompensated care, supports disease control, and allows access to public health facilities without generalising legal membership or broader social entitlement.
cases.included: Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.
cases.excluded: People without documents, people outside registration windows, refugees outside the relevant administrative channel, people unable to pay premiums, mobile workers, and people whose employer or status changes may remain outside or experience interrupted access.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.
sources: Migrant Workers and Migrants Health Insurance Fund
Implementing health insurance for migrants, Thailand

cases.title: Indonesia DTKS / SIKS-NG
cases.institutionsText: The Ministry of Social Affairs holds national responsibility, while local governments play a major role in verification, validation, updating, and local-level implementation.
populationGroups: Internally displaced people when captured by social welfare systems

transition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

mechanisms.scope: Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.
mechanisms.label: Portable entitlement

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Households are included when poverty, identity and domicile are administratively verified.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Central social-welfare architecture depends on local updating and validation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Registry inclusion controls programme exposure and can contract as readily as it expands.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Inclusion depends on whether households are visible, verified, updated, and classified within social welfare data systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households, informal settlers, people without current domicile documents, households newly impoverished by shocks, and households whose circumstances change between updating cycles may be missed or delayed.

cases.finding: DTKS and SIKS-NG can connect households to social assistance, but displaced households can fall out when emergency lists, domicile records, and registry updating cycles do not align.
cases.governanceFunction: The system makes eligibility administratively legible. It determines who can be found, verified, updated, and linked to programmes across central and local government. In displacement contexts, its governance function is not only targeting but continuity: whether vulnerability remains visible after movement.
cases.included: Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.
cases.excluded: Displaced households, informal settlers, people without current domicile documents, households newly impoverished by shocks, and households whose circumstances change between updating cycles may be missed or delayed.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.
sources: World Bank report on improving data quality for Indonesia's social registry

cases.title: Philippines 4Ps
cases.institutionsText: DSWD is the lead agency, with health, education, local government, payment providers, and community-level implementers carrying important implementation roles.
populationGroups: Internally displaced households when captured by programme systems

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.
mechanisms.label: Contingent financing

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Ordinary poverty eligibility can be extended administratively to disaster-affected households but displacement is not a permanent category.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: DSWD can expand an existing programme, but continuity depends on rules linking disaster response to regular enrolment.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: A statutory and budgeted programme can absorb temporary expansion, but longitudinal displacement costs remain bounded.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displaced households can be stabilized through an ordinary social protection programme, but conditionality, registry status, and mobility can reproduce exclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households may be missed if they are not in the targeting system, cannot maintain school or health compliance after moving, lack documentation, or cross local administrative boundaries without case transfer.

cases.finding: 4Ps gives the Philippines a statutory social protection platform, but displacement creates continuity risks when household location, school enrolment, health compliance, or registry status changes.
cases.governanceFunction: 4Ps links poor households to cash assistance and human capital conditions, using social welfare administration as a platform for recurring support rather than one-off emergency relief. In displacement contexts, it can function as a stabilising platform if records, payments, and compliance systems remain portable.
cases.included: Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.
cases.excluded: Displaced households may be missed if they are not in the targeting system, cannot maintain school or health compliance after moving, lack documentation, or cross local administrative boundaries without case transfer.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.
sources: Philippine legal e-library: Republic Act No. 11310

cases.title: ASEAN Agreement on Disaster Management and Emergency Response
cases.institutionsText: ASEAN member states carry primary responsibility. The AHA Centre, national disaster management offices, line ministries, and local authorities support coordination and implementation.
populationGroups: Communities at risk of disaster displacement

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Cross-institutional protocol

mechanisms.scope: Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Disaster-affected populations are recognized through event-based cooperation rather than a durable displacement status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional cooperation is strongest in preparedness and emergency response; recovery remains nationally implemented.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Regional cooperation does not establish durable fiscal responsibility for protracted displacement.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: ASEAN enables regional disaster cooperation, but assistance, recovery, and long-term inclusion depend on national and local systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected by conflict, persecution, development projects, slow-onset livelihood loss, or protracted post-disaster displacement may be weakly covered if their situation no longer fits emergency disaster response categories.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Regional cooperation with national implementation dependency

cases.finding: AADMER creates a binding regional disaster cooperation framework, but displacement continuity still depends on national systems, local authorities, and post-emergency recovery architecture.
cases.governanceFunction: AADMER regionalizes disaster management without replacing national responsibility. Its governance function is to coordinate assistance, preparedness, and response across ASEAN, while leaving protection, registration, social assistance, land, housing, and recovery responsibilities largely within domestic systems.
cases.included: Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.
cases.excluded: People affected by conflict, persecution, development projects, slow-onset livelihood loss, or protracted post-disaster displacement may be weakly covered if their situation no longer fits emergency disaster response categories.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.

cases.title: ASEAN Five-Point Consensus on Myanmar
cases.institutionsText: ASEAN member states, the ASEAN Chair, the Special Envoy, the AHA Centre, Myanmar authorities, and humanitarian actors all hold partial roles, but no single mechanism carries comprehensive protection responsibility.
populationGroups: Refugees and cross-border populations indirectly affected

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Conflict-affected civilians and displaced populations are indirectly included through the humanitarian assistance and dialogue dimensions, but not as rights-holders with enforceable claims.
mechanisms.label: Cross-institutional protocol

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Affected populations are addressed through humanitarian diplomacy without enforceable protection membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: ASEAN political consensus does not create operational authority sufficient to ensure access or continuity.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No durable financing or public-system absorption mechanism is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Regional diplomacy seeks humanitarian access and de-escalation while remaining constrained by sovereignty, consent, and limited enforcement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People in contested areas, cross-border populations, refugees, undocumented displaced people, and communities outside accessible channels may remain outside assistance and protection mechanisms.

cases.finding: The Five-Point Consensus provides ASEAN's political framework for Myanmar, but it has not created a reliable protection, access, or assistance architecture for displaced populations.
cases.governanceFunction: The consensus creates a diplomatic and humanitarian access framework. It is not an IDP policy, protection instrument, refugee framework, or social assistance system. Its implementation depends on political consent, access negotiation, ASEAN unity, and the conduct of conflict parties.
cases.included: Conflict-affected civilians and displaced populations are indirectly included through the humanitarian assistance and dialogue dimensions, but not as rights-holders with enforceable claims.
cases.excluded: People in contested areas, cross-border populations, refugees, undocumented displaced people, and communities outside accessible channels may remain outside assistance and protection mechanisms.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.
sources: ASEAN Chairman's Statement on the Leaders' Meeting, 24 April 2021
AP R2P report on ASEAN and the Myanmar humanitarian crisis

cases.title: Thailand Universal Coverage Scheme
cases.institutionsText: The National Health Security Office, Ministry of Public Health, public health facilities, and national budget authorities carry core responsibility.
populationGroups: Poor and vulnerable households indirectly protected through universal coverage

transition.trigger: status reclassification

interfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

mechanisms.scope: Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.
mechanisms.label: Recurrent budget integration

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Universal tax-financed entitlement is deep for citizens while non-citizens remain in separate schemes.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: A clear statutory purchaser and public delivery system sustain responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Tax financing converts health access into a recurrent national obligation for the covered population.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Health vulnerability is absorbed into a tax-financed universal system for citizens, while non-citizens remain governed through separate arrangements.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Non-citizens, undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, and some stateless or administratively unresolved populations are not automatically included through the citizen-based universal scheme, even if other arrangements may cover some groups.

cases.finding: Thailand's Universal Coverage Scheme demonstrates deep public-system absorption for citizens, while also revealing the categorical boundary separating citizen entitlement from non-citizen health inclusion.
cases.governanceFunction: The scheme converts health access into a public financing and entitlement function. For displacement governance, it is important because it shows what full fiscal and institutional absorption looks like when a population is recognized as part of the national health membership system.
cases.included: Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.
cases.excluded: Non-citizens, undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, and some stateless or administratively unresolved populations are not automatically included through the citizen-based universal scheme, even if other arrangements may cover some groups.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.
sources: Political economy of Thailand's tax-financed Universal Coverage Scheme

cases.title: Thailand Civil Registration and Statelessness Framework
cases.institutionsText: Interior authorities, civil registration offices, nationality authorities, local administrators, schools, health systems, and courts all shape recognition and access.
populationGroups: Non-citizens with temporary or special documentation

transition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

mechanisms.scope: Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Administrative identity creates graduated membership without necessarily resolving nationality.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Interior and civil-registration authorities control the gateway to multiple public systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Recognition may unlock services but does not itself guarantee recurrent benefit financing.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Legal identity systems make people visible to the state and create staged pathways from documentation toward fuller membership and service access.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without registration, people outside enumerated categories, mobile populations, unregistered children, and people whose records are incomplete or territorially tied may remain excluded or delayed.

cases.finding: Thailand's civil registration and statelessness framework can make non-citizens administratively visible, but service access and nationality pathways remain mediated by documentation category, residence history, and local administration.
cases.governanceFunction: The framework makes people administratively legible. It can connect identity, residence, nationality claims, education, health, and movement permissions, but it also creates category-specific pathways where rights depend heavily on documentation status.
cases.included: Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.
cases.excluded: People without registration, people outside enumerated categories, mobile populations, unregistered children, and people whose records are incomplete or territorially tied may remain excluded or delayed.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.
sources: UNHCR Thailand resolution of statelessness update
UNHCR press release on Thai Cabinet statelessness resolution
UNICEF and UNHCR on childhood statelessness in Thailand

cases.title: Indonesia Law No. 24/2007 on Disaster Management
cases.institutionsText: BNPB carries national coordination responsibility, while BPBDs, line ministries, local governments, and sectoral agencies carry implementation responsibilities.

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Statutory mandate assignment

mechanisms.scope: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Contingent financing

mechanisms.scope: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Institutional handoff

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when emergency beneficiary lists do not transfer into social protection, civil registration, housing, livelihood support, or local planning systems after the immediate response period.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when emergency beneficiary lists do not transfer into social protection, civil registration, housing, livelihood support, or local planning systems after the immediate response period.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is activated by disaster impact and emergency governance rather than displacement as a continuing condition.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: National emergency authority gives way to subnational recovery responsibility after the declared phase.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Extraordinary financing is time-bound; receiving districts absorb later costs through ordinary budgets.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: People become visible through disaster events and emergency administration, but continuity may weaken once the event-based response period ends.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People experiencing protracted displacement after the formal emergency phase, people moving outside the affected jurisdiction, informal settlers, and people affected by slow-onset or compound shocks may face gaps if they are no longer treated as disaster beneficiaries.

cases.finding: Indonesia's disaster management law creates a national-local disaster architecture, but displacement continuity depends on how emergency records connect to recovery, social protection, housing, and local administration.
cases.governanceFunction: The law structures disaster management as a public responsibility across national and regional authorities. In displacement contexts, it defines the disaster governance channel through which affected populations may be identified, assisted, evacuated, and supported during recovery.
cases.included: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.
cases.excluded: People experiencing protracted displacement after the formal emergency phase, people moving outside the affected jurisdiction, informal settlers, and people affected by slow-onset or compound shocks may face gaps if they are no longer treated as disaster beneficiaries.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when emergency beneficiary lists do not transfer into social protection, civil registration, housing, livelihood support, or local planning systems after the immediate response period.
sources: PreventionWeb summary of Indonesia Law No. 24/2007
Analysis of disaster emergency law in Indonesia

cases.title: Philippines Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act
cases.institutionsText: The National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, Office of Civil Defense, line agencies, local DRRM councils, and local government units carry major responsibilities.

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Statutory mandate assignment

mechanisms.scope: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Contingent financing

mechanisms.scope: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Intergovernmental fiscal transfer

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Disaster-affected populations are recognized through event and jurisdiction-based mechanisms.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The law creates a multi-level architecture spanning response and recovery, but no single actor owns longitudinal displacement.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: National and local disaster funds improve predictability but remain event-linked.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Disaster governance is institutionalized nationally but depends heavily on local governments whose fiscal and administrative capacity determines continuity.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households may fall out when they move across local government jurisdictions, remain displaced beyond emergency cycles, lack documentation, or cannot access local recovery programmes.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Decentralized responsibility with local fiscal exposure

cases.finding: The Philippines DRRM Act institutionalises disaster governance across national and local levels, but displacement continuity depends on whether local systems can carry assistance beyond evacuation and emergency response.
cases.governanceFunction: The Act decentralizes and institutionalizes disaster risk management. It connects preparedness, prevention, response, rehabilitation, and recovery to local government structures, making local capacity central to displacement outcomes.
cases.included: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
cases.excluded: Displaced households may fall out when they move across local government jurisdictions, remain displaced beyond emergency cycles, lack documentation, or cannot access local recovery programmes.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.

cases.title: Philippines Marawi Rehabilitation Architecture
cases.institutionsText: National agencies, Task Force Bangon Marawi, local government, BARMM institutions, housing agencies, social welfare bodies, public works agencies, and donors have all held roles.

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.
mechanisms.label: Institutional handoff

mechanisms.scope: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.
mechanisms.label: Cross-institutional protocol

mechanisms.scope: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.
mechanisms.label: Recurrent budget integration

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Conflict-displaced households are recognized through a rehabilitation architecture but differentiated by location and eligibility.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Responsibility fragments across housing, education, livelihoods and local recovery institutions as displacement becomes protracted.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Reconstruction appropriations are substantial but programme- and phase-bounded.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Inclusion is pursued through reconstruction, compensation, housing, and local recovery, but land, documentation, and accountability bottlenecks shape outcomes.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Households without complete land or property documentation, renters, informal occupants, people outside official lists, and families whose displacement becomes protracted may face exclusion or delay.

cases.finding: The Marawi recovery architecture shows how conflict displacement can shift from emergency response to reconstruction while many households remain caught between return, compensation, housing, and local recovery systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The architecture coordinates reconstruction, housing, services, compensation, infrastructure, and assistance for displaced and affected populations. Its governance challenge is to convert a conflict-recovery programme into durable urban inclusion.
cases.included: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.
cases.excluded: Households without complete land or property documentation, renters, informal occupants, people outside official lists, and families whose displacement becomes protracted may face exclusion or delay.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.
sources: Department of Finance: Bangon Marawi rehabilitation pledges
Bangon Marawi presentation hosted by National Economic and Development Authority
IDMC: Philippines six years after Marawi conflict
IOM: Marawi stories of escape, loss and hope

cases.title: Malaysia Foreign Worker Health Protection Scheme
cases.institutionsText: Health authorities, labour authorities, immigration agencies, employers, insurers, and public hospitals all shape access.

transition.trigger: status reclassification

interfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

mechanisms.scope: Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Contributory or premium-based inclusion

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Health access is tied to lawful employment and administrative registration rather than vulnerability.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Labour and health institutions cooperate while immigration status remains controlling.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Employer- or worker-linked financing bounds public exposure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Health access and cost management are tied to documented employment and insurance, leaving people outside labour channels structurally exposed.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, dependants outside employment-linked arrangements, people who lose work status, and people unable to pay charges may be excluded or face high barriers.

cases.finding: Malaysia's foreign worker health protection architecture links some migrant health access to employment and insurance, but refugees, undocumented migrants, and people outside labour channels remain structurally exposed.
cases.governanceFunction: The scheme manages health costs and access for documented foreign workers without creating general non-citizen health inclusion. Its function is to internalize some health risk into labour migration administration and employer-linked arrangements.
cases.included: Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.
cases.excluded: Undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, dependants outside employment-linked arrangements, people who lose work status, and people unable to pay charges may be excluded or face high barriers.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.

cases.title: Myanmar Maternal and Child Cash Transfer
cases.institutionsText: Myanmar's social welfare authorities, health and nutrition actors, local administrators, implementing partners, and development financiers have all played roles.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

mechanisms.scope: Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.
mechanisms.label: Recurrent budget integration

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Inclusion is framed through maternal and child vulnerability rather than displacement status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Social-welfare and health mandates permit bounded inclusion but depend on functioning state delivery.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Programme financing can become recurrent, but crisis and territorial fragmentation undermine continuity.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Early social protection infrastructure can reduce vulnerability, but crisis, conflict, and administrative disruption expose the fragility of programme continuity.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Households outside programme areas, displaced households not captured by registration, families moving across townships, and people in inaccessible or conflict-affected areas may be excluded.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Nascent social protection under crisis stress

cases.finding: Myanmar's MCCT shows how early social protection systems can create resilience before crisis, but conflict, economic collapse, and administrative disruption expose the fragility of programme continuity.
cases.governanceFunction: The programme links social protection, nutrition, human capital, and household resilience. In displacement governance terms, it shows how national social assistance can reduce vulnerability before shocks, but also how programme continuity depends on political and administrative stability.
cases.included: Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.
cases.excluded: Households outside programme areas, displaced households not captured by registration, families moving across townships, and people in inaccessible or conflict-affected areas may be excluded.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.
sources: World Bank press release on Myanmar MCCT financing
Journal of Nutrition study on sustained MCCT benefits
Save the Children / IGC presentation on MCCT

cases.title: Bangladesh-UNHCR Rohingya Registration and Identity Architecture
cases.institutionsText: The Government of Bangladesh, UNHCR, humanitarian agencies, camp authorities, and protection actors share operational roles, while durable solutions depend on broader political and regional processes.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Registration creates humanitarian legibility without full domestic legal membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Government and UNHCR share registration functions while service delivery remains largely humanitarian.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Financing is principally humanitarian and externally conditioned.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees are made visible for assistance and protection while remaining outside durable legal membership and ordinary national systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Unregistered new arrivals, people who avoid registration, people with contested data, people outside camp systems, and those whose status or identity records are disputed may face exclusion or restricted access.

cases.finding: The Bangladesh-UNHCR registration architecture gives Rohingya refugees operational identity for assistance and protection, but it does not create durable legal status, national inclusion, or portable membership.
cases.governanceFunction: The architecture makes the refugee population visible for humanitarian assistance, family linkage, protection casework, and service delivery. It also supports host-state population management without transforming refugees into members of national systems.
cases.included: Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.
cases.excluded: Unregistered new arrivals, people who avoid registration, people with contested data, people outside camp systems, and those whose status or identity records are disputed may face exclusion or restricted access.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.
sources: UNHCR: Joint Bangladesh/UNHCR verification gets underway
UNHCR: Rohingya refugees receive identity documents
UNHCR Operational Data Portal: Myanmar situation

cases.title: ASEAN Declaration on the Protection and Promotion of the Rights of Migrant Workers
cases.institutionsText: Sending and receiving states share responsibilities, with labour ministries, immigration authorities, recruitment regulators, employers, consulates, ASEAN bodies, and civil society all playing roles.

transition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.
mechanisms.label: Portable entitlement

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Protection is strongest for regular migrant workers and weakest for irregular or displaced populations.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional commitments depend on national labour and migration implementation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No regional financing obligation is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: ASEAN recognises migrant-worker protection while leaving enforcement, eligibility, and portability to national labour and immigration systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Undocumented workers, dependants, refugees working informally, trafficking survivors outside labour channels, and workers whose employment status changes may fall outside effective protection.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Managed labour mobility with bounded protection

cases.finding: The ASEAN migrant workers declaration recognizes migrant-worker protection as a regional concern, but access to rights remains mediated by national labour, immigration, recruitment, and social protection systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The declaration provides a regional cooperation framework for labour migration without creating directly enforceable individual entitlements. It links recruitment regulation, labour protection, documentation, welfare, and state cooperation, but leaves implementation to domestic law and bilateral or regional arrangements.
cases.included: Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.
cases.excluded: Undocumented workers, dependants, refugees working informally, trafficking survivors outside labour channels, and workers whose employment status changes may fall outside effective protection.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.
sources: ASEAN Declaration on Migrant Workers PDF
NUS Centre for International Law copy of the declaration

cases.title: Indonesia Presidential Regulation No. 125/2016 on the Handling of Refugees from Abroad
cases.institutionsText: The regulation assigns roles to central government, local government, immigration, police, rescue authorities, and relevant ministries, with UNHCR and international organizations playing major operational roles.

transition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Statutory mandate assignment

mechanisms.scope: Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.
mechanisms.label: Cross-institutional protocol

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugees are recognized as a distinct administrative caseload without general legal or socioeconomic inclusion.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The regulation assigns cross-sector roles but remains focused on handling rather than longitudinal public-system responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Implementation relies heavily on humanitarian and local arrangements rather than recurrent inclusion budgets.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees are administratively received, sheltered, and coordinated without being absorbed into a full asylum, work, or national inclusion system.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People outside official handling channels, undocumented migrants not recognized as asylum seekers, long-staying refugees needing work or education access, and people seeking durable local integration remain weakly covered.

cases.finding: Indonesia's refugee regulation creates an administrative framework for handling refugees from abroad, but it does not establish a full asylum system, durable status, or ordinary service inclusion.
cases.governanceFunction: The regulation organizes refugee handling as an administrative and coordination matter rather than a full status-determination or integration system.
