It clarifies roles for national and local authorities while leaving refugee protection heavily dependent on cooperation with UNHCR and IOM.
cases.included: Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.
cases.excluded: People outside official handling channels, undocumented migrants not recognized as asylum seekers, long-staying refugees needing work or education access, and people seeking durable local integration remain weakly covered.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.

Indonesia Cabinet Secretariat note on Regulation No. 125/2016

cases.title: Thailand Refugee Camp and Border Assistance Architecture
cases.institutionsText: Thai authorities, camp administration, UNHCR, NGOs, community-based organizations, donors, and refugee committees all carry roles within a constrained protection environment.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.
mechanisms.label: Cross-institutional protocol

mechanisms.scope: Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Camp populations are administratively tolerated and assisted through a territorially bounded humanitarian category.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Humanitarian actors carry service responsibility without a durable transfer into national systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: External humanitarian finance substitutes for recurrent state expenditure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees receive protection and assistance in bounded camp spaces while movement, work, legal status, and national-system access remain constrained.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Urban asylum seekers, unregistered arrivals, people outside camps, refugees who move for work, and people whose status is not recognized may have weaker access to assistance and protection.

cases.finding: Thailand's border camp architecture has provided long-term refuge and assistance for Myanmar refugees, but protection remains territorially bounded and weakly connected to ordinary national systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The system manages refugee protection through humanitarian containment rather than national-system absorption. It provides safety and assistance in designated areas while preserving the state's discretion over status, movement, work, and long-term integration.
cases.included: Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.
cases.excluded: Urban asylum seekers, unregistered arrivals, people outside camps, refugees who move for work, and people whose status is not recognized may have weaker access to assistance and protection.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.

cases.title: Philippines Migrant Workers and Overseas Filipinos Act
cases.institutionsText: The Philippine state, labour migration agencies, consulates, welfare agencies, recruitment regulators, employers, destination-country authorities, and legal assistance systems share roles.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Statutory mandate assignment

mechanisms.scope: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Portable entitlement

mechanisms.scope: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
mechanisms.label: Lead-agency responsibility

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Citizenship and formal overseas-worker status anchor strong transnational protection.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Dedicated institutions maintain responsibility beyond national territory.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Contributory and public welfare mechanisms bound support to a recognized worker category.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The state extends protection to nationals abroad through labour migration, consular, welfare, repatriation, and reintegration systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Workers outside documented channels, irregular migrants, people whose status changes abroad, dependants in complex situations, and migrants in inaccessible locations may face weaker practical protection.

cases.finding: The Philippines migrant workers law builds a transnational protection architecture for overseas workers, but its portability depends on recruitment regulation, consular reach, employer compliance, and destination-country systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The law extends state protection beyond national territory through labour migration governance. It links recruitment regulation, deployment, welfare funds, legal assistance, consular protection, and repatriation into a transnational administrative system.
cases.included: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
cases.excluded: Workers outside documented channels, irregular migrants, people whose status changes abroad, dependants in complex situations, and migrants in inaccessible locations may face weaker practical protection.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.
sources: University of Minnesota Human Rights Library: Republic Act No. 10022

cases.title: Vietnam Residence Law and Household Registration Reform
cases.institutionsText: Public security authorities, local administrations, population database managers, service agencies, and administrative procedure offices carry implementation responsibilities.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

mechanisms.scope: Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.
mechanisms.label: Portable entitlement

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Citizen access is mediated through residence registration and digital administrative identity.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Public-security registration architecture remains the gateway to locally delivered services.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Local service budgets remain territorially allocated even as registration becomes more portable.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Digital residence data can reduce paper-based exclusion, but access still depends on local registration, data accuracy, and administrative acceptance.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without stable lawful residence, informal migrants, renters unable to meet local registration requirements, and people whose digital records are incomplete or outdated may continue to face barriers.

cases.finding: Vietnam's residence reform reduces reliance on paper household registration, but access to services still depends on how digital residence data translate into portable administrative inclusion.
cases.governanceFunction: The reform modernizes the administrative infrastructure that connects people to place, services, and legal transactions. In displacement terms, it is relevant because residence registration can either enable or block access when people move internally.
cases.included: Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.
cases.excluded: People without stable lawful residence, informal migrants, renters unable to meet local registration requirements, and people whose digital records are incomplete or outdated may continue to face barriers.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.
sources: Vietnam Law Magazine: management of citizen residence to be digitalized

cases.title: Cambodia Land Law and Economic Land Concession Architecture
cases.institutionsText: Land ministries, cadastral authorities, local governments, concession-granting authorities, courts, investors, and dispute resolution bodies shape implementation.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

interfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.
mechanisms.label: Grievance and review

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Formal title and legally recognized use determine whether affected people enter compensation and remedy systems.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Land and concession institutions prioritize investment administration, with fragmented post-displacement responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Compensation is transaction- or project-bounded rather than integrated into continuing public support.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Formal land law protects recognized claims while communities with weak, informal, or contested tenure remain vulnerable to investment-driven displacement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Informal settlers, people without formal documents, communities on contested state land, renters, mobile groups, and households affected by concession-related pressures may be weakly protected.

cases.finding: Cambodia's land law provides a legal architecture for ownership and concessions, but displacement risk emerges where tenure recognition, concession governance, and compensation systems fail to protect affected communities.
cases.governanceFunction: The law structures the relationship between land, state authority, private investment, community tenure, and compensation. In displacement terms, it determines who is recognized as having protectable land rights when development, concessions, or state land allocation affect communities.
cases.included: People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.
cases.excluded: Informal settlers, people without formal documents, communities on contested state land, renters, mobile groups, and households affected by concession-related pressures may be weakly protected.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.
sources: Council for the Development of Cambodia: Land

cases.title: Lao PDR Decree on Compensation and Resettlement Management in Development Projects
cases.institutionsText: Project developers, responsible ministries, local authorities, environmental and social agencies, and project implementation bodies share roles in compensation, resettlement, and monitoring.
populationGroups: People physically displaced by development projects
People economically displaced by development projects

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

interfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People and communities affected by development projects, including those facing loss of land, assets, income, livelihoods, or relocation, may be included through compensation and resettlement procedures.
mechanisms.label: Project-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: People and communities affected by development projects, including those facing loss of land, assets, income, livelihoods, or relocation, may be included through compensation and resettlement procedures.
mechanisms.label: Post-project responsibility

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when compensation is paid but livelihoods are not restored, when relocated communities lose access to land or services, or when monitoring ends before social and economic recovery is achieved.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when compensation is paid but livelihoods are not restored, when relocated communities lose access to land or services, or when monitoring ends before social and economic recovery is achieved.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Project-affected status creates bounded eligibility for compensation and resettlement.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Project implementers hold responsibility during implementation, with weak transfer to district systems afterward.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Support is project-bounded and difficult to absorb into recurrent local budgets.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displacement is managed through project compensation and resettlement procedures, with livelihood restoration dependent on implementation quality and monitoring.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People with informal or undocumented land use, indirectly affected households, downstream communities, seasonal users, and people whose livelihood losses persist after relocation may be weakly covered.

cases.finding: Lao PDR's resettlement decree recognizes people affected by development projects, but long-term inclusion depends on whether compensation and relocation restore livelihoods, services, and community continuity.
cases.governanceFunction: The decree creates a domestic framework for managing project-induced displacement. It links development approval, compensation, resettlement planning, monitoring, and affected-person protection.
cases.included: People and communities affected by development projects, including those facing loss of land, assets, income, livelihoods, or relocation, may be included through compensation and resettlement procedures.
cases.excluded: People with informal or undocumented land use, indirectly affected households, downstream communities, seasonal users, and people whose livelihood losses persist after relocation may be weakly covered.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when compensation is paid but livelihoods are not restored, when relocated communities lose access to land or services, or when monitoring ends before social and economic recovery is achieved.
sources: Decree No. 84 on Compensation and Resettlement Management in Development Projects PDF
Land Portal entry on Lao PDR compensation and resettlement decree
World Bank resettlement policy framework referencing Decree 84

cases.title: Thailand Cabinet Resolution on Accelerated Statelessness Resolution
cases.institutionsText: The Ministry of Interior, district offices, civil registration authorities, nationality authorities, local administrators, schools, health systems, and supporting partners shape implementation.
populationGroups: Children born in Thailand without nationality

transition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Registered stateless persons meeting eligibility conditions, including long-term residents and children born in Thailand who may qualify for nationality or status resolution, are the core population.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: Registered stateless persons meeting eligibility conditions, including long-term residents and children born in Thailand who may qualify for nationality or status resolution, are the core population.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when registration records do not translate into status decisions, when local verification is uneven, or when nationality pathways do not immediately unlock full service, mobility, work, and social protection access.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when registration records do not translate into status decisions, when local verification is uneven, or when nationality pathways do not immediately unlock full service, mobility, work, and social protection access.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Administrative regularization expands pathways toward legal membership for defined stateless groups.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Cabinet-driven acceleration relies on interior and local registration implementation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: The measure primarily changes status and administrative access rather than creating a new benefit liability.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Administrative records are used to move registered stateless people toward legal status, nationality, and fuller access to ordinary systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People not registered, people with incomplete documentation, mobile populations, people outside recognized categories, and those unable to navigate local verification may remain excluded or delayed.

cases.finding: Thailand's accelerated statelessness resolution shows how administrative data can be converted into legal status pathways, but inclusion still depends on eligibility, local verification, and implementation capacity.
cases.governanceFunction: The resolution converts a long-standing administrative population into a legal status and nationality pathway. Its governance function is to move from visibility in state records toward fuller membership and access to rights.
cases.included: Registered stateless persons meeting eligibility conditions, including long-term residents and children born in Thailand who may qualify for nationality or status resolution, are the core population.
cases.excluded: People not registered, people with incomplete documentation, mobile populations, people outside recognized categories, and those unable to navigate local verification may remain excluded or delayed.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when registration records do not translate into status decisions, when local verification is uneven, or when nationality pathways do not immediately unlock full service, mobility, work, and social protection access.
sources: UNHCR press release on Thai Cabinet statelessness resolution
UNHCR Thailand update on resolution of legal status
UNICEF and UNHCR on childhood statelessness in Thailand

cases.title: UNHCR Registration as Humanitarian Identity Infrastructure
cases.institutionsText: UNHCR, host governments, registration partners, data protection officers, humanitarian agencies, and service providers all hold roles depending on the operational context.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: People who are able and eligible to register with UNHCR or a UNHCR-supported registration process may be included in protection and assistance systems.
mechanisms.label: Legal or administrative recognition

mechanisms.scope: People who are able and eligible to register with UNHCR or a UNHCR-supported registration process may be included in protection and assistance systems.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when UNHCR records do not interoperate with national civil registration, health, education, social protection, labour, or immigration systems, or when host states do not recognize UNHCR documentation for access.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when UNHCR records do not interoperate with national civil registration, health, education, social protection, labour, or immigration systems, or when host states do not recognize UNHCR documentation for access.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Humanitarian recognition creates a protection caseload but may not produce domestic legal membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: UNHCR controls registration where states have not absorbed the function.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Registration and linked assistance are externally financed and can remain parallel.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: UNHCR registration makes people visible for protection and assistance but does not automatically create domestic legal status or public-system inclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People who cannot access registration, fear registration, lack documentation, are outside operational areas, or fall outside UNHCR's mandate or prioritization may remain invisible.

cases.finding: UNHCR registration can make displaced people visible for protection and assistance, but it does not automatically create domestic legal status, public service access, or fiscal responsibility.
cases.governanceFunction: Registration creates humanitarian legibility. It identifies people, households, vulnerabilities, claims, assistance needs, and protection risks, while supporting casework, aid delivery, referral, documentation, and durable solutions processes.
cases.included: People who are able and eligible to register with UNHCR or a UNHCR-supported registration process may be included in protection and assistance systems.
cases.excluded: People who cannot access registration, fear registration, lack documentation, are outside operational areas, or fall outside UNHCR's mandate or prioritization may remain invisible.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when UNHCR records do not interoperate with national civil registration, health, education, social protection, labour, or immigration systems, or when host states do not recognize UNHCR documentation for access.
sources: UNHCR registration and identity management guidance
UNHCR Thailand registration and documents page

cases.title: Humanitarian Cash Transfer Systems as Parallel Social Protection
cases.institutionsText: Humanitarian agencies, donors, cash working groups, governments, social protection ministries, payment providers, financial institutions, and local authorities may all shape delivery.

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

mechanisms.scope: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
mechanisms.label: Externally financed system expansion

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Humanitarian vulnerability criteria create a separate caseload from ordinary social protection.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Humanitarian agencies retain delivery responsibility unless an explicit transition is designed.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Short-term external finance permits coverage without creating recurrent state liability.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Humanitarian cash can stabilize households quickly, but without alignment to national systems it remains a parallel residual rather than sustainable inclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People outside humanitarian targeting, people without registration, households in inaccessible areas, people excluded by documentation or payment requirements, and households whose needs persist after programme closure may be left out.

cases.finding: Humanitarian cash can stabilize displaced households quickly, but without alignment to national systems it can remain a parallel residual rather than a bridge to sustainable inclusion.
cases.governanceFunction: Humanitarian cash converts emergency need into flexible household support. Its governance importance lies in whether cash systems remain parallel or align with national social protection registries, payment rails, shock-response protocols, and grievance mechanisms.
cases.included: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
cases.excluded: People outside humanitarian targeting, people without registration, households in inaccessible areas, people excluded by documentation or payment requirements, and households whose needs persist after programme closure may be left out.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.
sources: OECD Social Protection for the Forcibly Displaced

cases.title: ADB Safeguard Policy Statement and Environmental and Social Framework
cases.institutionsText: Borrowers and project executing agencies carry implementation responsibility, with ADB due diligence, supervision, disclosure, and compliance oversight.

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

interfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.
mechanisms.label: Project-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.
mechanisms.label: Grievance and review

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is tied to project impact and safeguard classifications.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Borrower and project obligations are supervised through the financing relationship.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Mitigation is financed within project envelopes and does not automatically persist after closure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Development-induced displacement is governed through project safeguards, mitigation plans, borrower obligations, and lender supervision.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected indirectly, cumulatively, outside the project area, after project closure, or through broader market and territorial effects may be harder to capture unless the assessment and mitigation design include them.

cases.finding: ADB safeguards govern displacement within financed projects, but continuity depends on whether resettlement and livelihood restoration connect to ordinary public systems after project implementation.
cases.governanceFunction: The framework translates development finance into obligations for risk identification, impact assessment, compensation, consultation, resettlement planning, livelihood restoration, grievance redress, and monitoring. In displacement terms, it governs project-created displacement as an environmental and social risk attached to finance.
cases.included: People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.
cases.excluded: People affected indirectly, cumulatively, outside the project area, after project closure, or through broader market and territorial effects may be harder to capture unless the assessment and mitigation design include them.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.

cases.title: World Bank IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees
cases.institutionsText: Host governments, World Bank teams, IDA governance structures, implementing agencies, local authorities, and partner institutions share responsibility through project preparation and implementation.

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.
mechanisms.label: Externally financed system expansion

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.
mechanisms.label: Recurrent budget integration

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility depends on refugee-hosting status, policy commitments and country access to concessional finance.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Finance can strengthen government ownership where national systems accept refugee inclusion.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Concessional resources reduce the marginal cost of absorption but remain externally conditioned.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugee-hosting costs are partially absorbed into development finance where host-state policy commitments and concessional financing align.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Refugees in countries outside eligibility criteria, urban refugees not captured in project areas, undocumented populations, IDPs, and non-refugee displaced groups may fall outside unless project design includes them through other channels.

cases.finding: The IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees helps turn refugee hosting into a development-finance issue, but inclusion depends on host-state policy, project design, and whether financing strengthens ordinary systems.
cases.governanceFunction: The window shifts part of refugee response from humanitarian appeals into development finance. It supports investments in services, livelihoods, institutions, infrastructure, and policy environments that benefit both refugees and host communities.
cases.included: Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.
cases.excluded: Refugees in countries outside eligibility criteria, urban refugees not captured in project areas, undocumented populations, IDPs, and non-refugee displaced groups may fall outside unless project design includes them through other channels.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.
sources: World Bank IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees
World Bank factsheet: 10 things to know about WHR
IDA18 Regional Sub-Window for Refugees and Host Communities

cases.title: Global Concessional Financing Facility
cases.institutionsText: Host governments borrow and implement, partner multilateral development banks prepare and supervise projects, donors provide concessionality, and the World Bank hosts the facility secretariat.

transition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities may benefit where eligible middle-income host countries use GCFF-supported financing for services, infrastructure, livelihoods, health, education, or local development.
mechanisms.label: Externally financed system expansion

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee-hosting middle-income countries qualify through crisis and facility criteria rather than permanent entitlement.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Financing supports host-government programmes but does not itself assign sustained sectoral responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Subsidized borrowing lowers financing costs while preserving sovereign debt liability.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Donor-funded concessionality lowers borrowing costs for refugee-hosting states but still works through sovereign loans and project logic.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Countries outside eligibility, displaced populations not framed as refugees, undocumented populations, IDPs, and people outside project areas may remain outside the facility's reach.

cases.finding: The GCFF lowers the financing cost of refugee-hosting development projects, but it still operates through sovereign borrowing and project logic rather than automatic inclusion entitlements.
cases.governanceFunction: The facility converts refugee hosting into a concessional finance problem. It recognizes that middle-income countries may face major refugee-related costs but lack access to the same concessional terms available to low-income countries.
cases.included: Refugees and host communities may benefit where eligible middle-income host countries use GCFF-supported financing for services, infrastructure, livelihoods, health, education, or local development.
cases.excluded: Countries outside eligibility, displaced populations not framed as refugees, undocumented populations, IDPs, and people outside project areas may remain outside the facility's reach.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.
sources: Global Concessional Financing Facility official website
World Bank FIF Trustee GCFF page
World Bank results feature on GCFF

cases.title: OECD Social Protection for the Forcibly Displaced
cases.institutionsText: Host governments, donors, social protection ministries, humanitarian agencies, development partners, UN agencies, and local authorities all shape implementation.
populationGroups: Forcibly displaced people in low- and middle-income countries

transition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.
mechanisms.label: Sectoral absorption

mechanisms.scope: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

mechanisms.scope: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.
mechanisms.label: Recurrent budget integration

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: The framework distinguishes de jure inclusion from actual access for refugees and IDPs.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: It diagnoses the institutions required for inclusion but is not itself an implementing mandate.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: It identifies sustainable financing as a condition rather than creating a financing mechanism.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Legal or policy access to social protection may exist, but actual inclusion depends on documentation, data, financing, delivery capacity, and politics.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside where social protection systems are nascent, underfinanced, citizenship-tied, documentation-dependent, or not designed for mobile and displaced populations.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: De jure inclusion with de facto exclusion

cases.finding: The OECD/EBA framework shows that legal access to social protection often exists on paper, while actual inclusion depends on data, financing, administrative capacity, and political economy.
cases.governanceFunction: The paper functions as an analytical policy framework for inclusion. It connects forced displacement to social protection architecture, showing that access depends on law, delivery systems, registration, financing, host-state capacity, and donor support.
cases.included: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.
cases.excluded: People may remain outside where social protection systems are nascent, underfinanced, citizenship-tied, documentation-dependent, or not designed for mobile and displaced populations.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.

cases.title: World Bank Adaptive Social Protection Framework
cases.institutionsText: Social protection ministries, finance ministries, disaster management agencies, local governments, development partners, and payment or data providers share implementation responsibilities.
populationGroups: Displaced people when included in social protection systems

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

interfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
mechanisms.label: Contingent financing

mechanisms.scope: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
mechanisms.label: Emergency-to-system transition

mechanisms.scope: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
mechanisms.label: Registry interoperability

continuity.explanation: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.

cases.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Shock-affected households can be included when registries and programme rules recognize them.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Social-protection institutions retain control while disaster and finance actors provide triggers and resources.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Contingent finance enables expansion but may not sustain post-displacement needs.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Social protection can scale during shocks, but displaced people benefit only if registries, financing, triggers, and payment systems can reach them.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households, refugees, informal migrants, undocumented people, and people outside registry systems may remain excluded unless adaptive systems are explicitly designed to include them.

cases.finding: Adaptive social protection strengthens systems for shocks, but displacement inclusion depends on whether registries, financing, and delivery rules can follow people across place and status.
cases.governanceFunction: The framework links social protection to resilience, disaster risk management, climate adaptation, and crisis response. For displacement governance, its function is to show how ordinary systems can scale, adjust, and respond before humanitarian need becomes entrenched.
cases.included: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
cases.excluded: Displaced households, refugees, informal migrants, undocumented people, and people outside registry systems may remain excluded unless adaptive systems are explicitly designed to include them.
cases.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.

cases.title: Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction
cases.institutionsText: States hold primary responsibility, supported by local governments, disaster management agencies, UNDRR, civil society, the private sector, and international cooperation.

transition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Implementation depends on domestic disaster-risk institutions and plans.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: The framework promotes investment in risk reduction but creates no direct fiscal entitlement.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displacement is addressed upstream through risk reduction, preparedness, resilience, and recovery planning rather than individual entitlement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People displaced beyond the emergency phase, cross-border disaster-displaced people, people outside local plans, informal settlers, and mobile populations may be left out if DRR systems do not explicitly address displacement.

cases.finding: The Sendai Framework strengthens disaster risk governance, but displacement protection depends on whether risk reduction, evacuation, recovery, and social systems are designed around people who move.
cases.governanceFunction: The framework shifts disaster policy from response toward prevention, risk governance, resilience investment, and recovery. In displacement governance, it provides the prevention and risk-reduction architecture that can reduce disaster displacement and shape post-disaster recovery.
cases.included: Communities and households exposed to disaster risk, disaster-affected populations, vulnerable groups, and people affected by recovery and reconstruction processes may be included through national DRR strategies.

countries.name: Cambodia
countries.overview: The primary mobility issue is labour outmigration to Thailand, while internal displacement is driven largely by land concessions, infrastructure, and dam construction. Refugee law exists but is weakly implemented. Flood and drought governance exists through NCDM, but internal displacement is not recognized through a dedicated longitudinal framework. Climate and Mekong hydrology pressures increase displacement risk. IDPoor and Health Equity Funds provide important targeting and health-financing mechanisms, but commune-based administration and three-year update cycles create portability and timing gaps for displaced households. Coordination is often driven by development priorities, party-state control, and project management units rather than a protection-oriented displacement framework. Displacement-related support is commonly handled through project compensation, donor-supported programmes, or locally administered services rather than portable fiscal responsibility. Update (Jun 2026): IDPoor, Cambodia's national poverty/social-protection registry, has moved from a fixed three-year survey cycle to on-demand digital registration -- piloted in 2017, scaled nationally from 2020 under COVID-19 pressure, and now formalized by the 2023 Law on Civil Registration, Vital Statistics, and Identification (CRVSID), with a biometric, QR-verified eID rolling out from 2025. The previous registry-cycle gap (households missing a survey round had to wait years for the next) has been substantially narrowed for IDPoor specifically, though the broader question of cross-system interoperability (IDPoor vs. civil registration vs. other sectoral databases) and whether mobile or displaced households can register on demand at their actual location remains less clear from public documentation. The existing characterization should be checked against whether it is describing this current on-demand architecture or the earlier fixed-cycle system. Infrastructure displacement is central. Lower Sesan 2 shows how compensation and relocation packages can be provided while service access and vulnerability continue beyond the project boundary.

assessments.synthesis: The primary mobility issue is labour outmigration to Thailand, while internal displacement is driven largely by land concessions, infrastructure, and dam construction. Refugee law exists but is weakly implemented.

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.overview: Indonesia is primarily a labour-sending state but has a formal refugee-handling instrument. Presidential Regulation 125/2016 assigns state responsibilities but does not create integration pathways or resolve local fiscal implementation gaps. BNPB provides strong emergency coordination, but support is time-bound. When emergency status expires, responsibility shifts to local government budgets, creating gaps for households whose displacement continues. Indonesia has significant social protection and health-system capacity through PKH, SIKS-NG, and BPJS Kesehatan, including adaptive enrolment in crises. The problem is not system absence but portability and eligibility continuity. Formal whole-of-government design exists for refugees and disasters, but implementation is fragmented through decentralised fiscal and administrative authority across hundreds of districts. The fiscal constraint is jurisdictional. Emergency budgets can activate rapidly, but longer-term responsibility must be absorbed by local governments whose budget ceilings do not automatically follow displaced populations. The e-KTP and SIKS-NG architecture is highly developed but territorially anchored. Disaster databases and social protection registries do not automatically interoperate, so displaced households can be reclassified or excluded at re-enumeration. Infrastructure and development displacement are present but less central than disaster and data-system cases in the current chapter. The same absorption constraints would apply where project-affected populations require transfer into national systems.

countries.name: Lao PDR
countries.overview: Mobility governance is dominated by labour emigration to Thailand and state-directed resettlement of highland communities. Inward refugee governance is limited despite formal Convention accession. Disaster governance exists but remains weaker than in larger neighbours. The Attapeu dam collapse exposed the interface between disaster response, infrastructure liability, and displaced-community support. National social protection infrastructure is limited, especially in rural and resettled areas. Health, education, and welfare access often depend on village location and project-specific support. Whole-of-government coherence is organized around development and infrastructure priorities. Resettlement is handled through project bodies and line ministries rather than a cross-sectoral IDP protection mandate. Displacement support is usually project financed and bounded by resettlement action plans. Limited ordinary social protection capacity makes fiscal embedding difficult after project closure. Administrative and social protection data systems are less developed than in Indonesia, Vietnam, or the Philippines. Project records rarely provide a pathway into broader social support. Hydropower and corridor infrastructure are central. Xayaburi illustrates the mismatch between safeguard-calibrated project obligations and displacement as a longitudinal, cross-border livelihood condition.

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.overview: Update (Jun 2026): in Jan 2026 the government began rolling out Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), a government-run registration system developed by MIMOS Berhad that is intended to replace UNHCR's registration role outright. The Home Minister has stated DPP will be the sole recognized refugee documentation, with UNHCR's function narrowed to resettlement referrals only. This is a different mechanism from non-recognition: rather than leaving refugees outside any status system, the state is inserting itself as the sole registering authority while still declining to ratify the 1951 Convention or legislate refugee status. DPP cards do not confer legal status or a right to remain; they are framed as bridging documentation pending resettlement, in a context where resettlement departures fell sharply (1,970 in 2025, down from 8,627 in 2024) after US resettlement cuts. Mass enforcement has intensified in parallel (approx. 92,000 irregular-migrant arrests in 2025 vs 12,000 in 2021), including arrests of UNHCR cardholders, so the categorical fault line persists even as its administrative form changes. Disaster management is organized around national disaster response but does not systematically include non-citizen or undocumented populations. Internal displacement is not a significant recognized policy category. Social protection programmes are overwhelmingly citizen or lawful-worker based. Refugees can access some health services at foreign-patient rates but are excluded from ordinary assistance, social insurance, and formal labour access. Update (Jun 2026): the Home Affairs Ministry, via the national applied-research body MIMOS Berhad, has taken direct operational control of refugee registration through DPP, a function previously performed by UNHCR. This deepens immigration-led governance rather than diversifying it: a security- and documentation-control rationale (curbing fake documentation, responding to a National Security Council directive) now sits alongside the pre-existing immigration-enforcement mandate, with no separate protection-mandated institution holding parallel authority. SUHAKAM, Malaysia's human rights commission, has publicly criticized the secrecy of the underlying NSC Directive No. 23 as insufficient and untimely. Fiscal architecture reinforces exclusion by reserving social assistance and insurance systems for citizens or documented workers. Refugee inclusion is framed as potential fiscal and membership exposure rather than resilience investment. Update (Jun 2026): DPP collects biometrics into a government-controlled database, replacing the UNHCR-issued refugee card as the operative identification instrument for those registered. This is the first instance of Malaysia building its own state-held refugee identity system rather than relying on (and periodically disregarding) UNHCR documentation -- a shift from 'parallel identification without state effect' toward state-controlled identification without state-conferred status. No published data-protection safeguards, retention limits, or appeal mechanism have been identified for the system as of mid-2026; HRW wrote the Home Affairs Ministry on 21 Apr 2026 requesting clarification and did not receive a reply. Development-induced displacement is not the central mapped governance problem, though Orang Asli and land-related displacement raise protection questions outside refugee and migration systems.

countries.name: Myanmar
countries.overview: Migration and displacement are shaped by state-produced statelessness, conflict, labour emigration, and parallel post-coup governance. The 1982 Citizenship Law remains the core categorical exclusion mechanism for Rohingya. Update (Jun 2026): the Mar 2025 magnitude-7.7 earthquake compounded conflict-driven displacement, damaging tens of thousands of houses and nearly 70 health facilities and disrupting agriculture across central Myanmar. OCHA's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan projects internal displacement rising from approximately 3.6 million to 4 million during 2026, and -- reflecting funding contraction rather than reduced need -- narrowed its operational scope to life-saving and protection activities in conflict- and earthquake-affected townships (about two-thirds of the country) rather than a nationwide caseload. This reinforces rather than changes the existing 'institutional reversal' characterization, but the earthquake and the 2026 scope narrowing are significant, undocumented additions: disaster and conflict displacement are now compounding in the same response plan, and humanitarian financing itself is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded) at the same time displacement needs are rising. Pre-coup social protection expansion through MCCT, social pensions, and disability allowances collapsed after 2021. Health, education, and registration systems have fragmented into state, resistance, and humanitarian channels. Whole-of-government coordination is structurally impossible under competing authorities and conflict. The mapping therefore reads Myanmar less as an absorption case than as a reconstruction requirements case. Update (Jun 2026): beyond the domestic fiscal collapse this cell already describes, the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan's narrowed scope is itself a fiscal-architecture data point: international humanitarian financing for Myanmar is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was met) at precisely the moment the Mar 2025 earthquake has compounded conflict-driven need. Myanmar functions here as a limit case not only for domestic fiscal embedding but for the proposition -- central to the chapter's broader argument -- that humanitarian financing cannot substitute indefinitely for sovereign fiscal capacity; where both have collapsed simultaneously, no financing architecture currently absorbs the gap. IDP data are dispersed across humanitarian registration systems, ethnic governance structures, and fragmented state records. No institutionalized transfer mechanism exists into stable national systems. Infrastructure-related displacement exists, but post-coup conflict and institutional collapse dominate the governance landscape. Development finance entry points are constrained by legitimacy, sanctions, and access.

This reinforces rather than changes the existing 'institutional reversal' characterization, but the earthquake and the 2026 scope narrowing are significant, undocumented additions: disaster and conflict displacement are now compounding in the same response plan, and humanitarian financing itself is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded) at the same time displacement needs are rising.

Pre-coup social protection expansion through MCCT, social pensions, and disability allowances collapsed after 2021. Health, education, and registration systems have fragmented into state, resistance, and humanitarian channels.

Whole-of-government coordination is structurally impossible under competing authorities and conflict. The mapping therefore reads Myanmar less as an absorption case than as a reconstruction requirements case.

Update (Jun 2026): beyond the domestic fiscal collapse this cell already describes, the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan's narrowed scope is itself a fiscal-architecture data point: international humanitarian financing for Myanmar is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was met) at precisely the moment the Mar 2025 earthquake has compounded conflict-driven need. Myanmar functions here as a limit case not only for domestic fiscal embedding but for the proposition -- central to the chapter's broader argument -- that humanitarian financing cannot substitute indefinitely for sovereign fiscal capacity; where both have collapsed simultaneously, no financing architecture currently absorbs the gap.

IDP data are dispersed across humanitarian registration systems, ethnic governance structures, and fragmented state records. No institutionalized transfer mechanism exists into stable national systems.

assessments.dimension: Housing, land, resettlement and development finance
assessments.synthesis: Infrastructure-related displacement exists, but post-coup conflict and institutional collapse dominate the governance landscape. Development finance entry points are constrained by legitimacy, sanctions, and access.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.overview: Outward labour migration is governed through robust longitudinal institutions, while inward migration and domestic displacement receive far less portable protection. This contrast exposes the architecture of membership and portability. Update (Jun 2026): the 2025 Anticipatory Action Law institutionalized pre-disaster planning and financing, a direct legislative response to the temporal drop-off this cell identifies (disaster response strong at activation, weaker on sustained financing into recovery). Whether the law closes the temporal gap or only moves it earlier in the disaster cycle (pre-positioning finance but still lacking sustained post-disaster transfer mechanisms) is not yet clear from public documentation and is worth tracking as implementation data emerges. Marawi compensation and temporary-shelter problems persist eight years on (approx. 8,200 people still displaced as of Mar 2025, paying rent on privately-owned land where shelters were built), confirming the existing characterization of activation without durable absorption rather than displacing it. 4Ps, Listahanan, PhilHealth, DepEd, and DSWD offer strong institutional capacity, but access is interrupted when displaced households cross municipal boundaries or fall between enumeration cycles. Whole-of-government disaster coordination is formalized, yet recovery responsibility fragments across housing, education, health, welfare, and local government mandates. No single institution carries longitudinal IDP responsibility. The Philippines demonstrates both fiscal integration and its limits. 4Ps is legislated and budgeted, but devolved fiscal architecture creates portability gaps when displaced populations move across municipalities. Listahanan provides a strong registry backbone and post-Haiyan data-matching precedent, but enumeration cycles and cross-municipal moves can interrupt benefits for displaced households. Infrastructure finance is less central than disaster and conflict displacement in the chapter’s Philippines analysis, though reconstruction and housing after Marawi show how projectized recovery can fragment responsibility.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.overview: Update (Jun 2026): the Aug 2025 cabinet resolution and Oct 2025 implementation granted work permits to long-staying Myanmar refugees in the nine border camps; the Royal Thai Government began issuing biometric, non-Thai ID cards in Jun 2026 that formally recognise camp residents as "Myanmar Displaced Persons" and register them in the national civil registry. This is the most significant formalisation of camp-based refugees' status since the camps were established in the 1980s, moving a defined cohort from administrative tolerance toward documented status with civil-registry linkage. The categorical fault line narrows for this cohort but does not close: status remains non-citizen, phased by age and employment, and distinct from asylum recognition or the National Screening Mechanism. Non-refoulement practice remains inconsistent, as the Feb 2025 deportation of long-detained Uyghur asylum seekers illustrates. Disaster governance is comparatively institutionalized through the Ministry of Interior, but internal displacement is not governed through a distinct longitudinal framework. Stateless and border populations are managed through registration instruments that can restrict movement and reset access across provinces. Update (Jun 2026): civil-registry integration accompanying the new ID cards is designed to enable access to banking, telecommunications, and healthcare for the camp-based cohort, alongside continued HICS coverage. This adds a second, registry-linked absorption channel alongside the existing premium-based HICS model, though it remains bounded to a defined and phased population rather than a general extension of public-system access to all non-Thai displaced persons. Update (Jun 2026): the cabinet-level work-rights resolution (2025) and the civil-registry ID rollout (2026) were both executive/administrative actions rather than legislative reform, consistent with the existing pattern of sectoral and cabinet-discretionary coordination. The Ministry of Labour (work permits) and Ministry of Interior (civil registration) are now both load-bearing for this cohort's status, alongside the pre-existing screening and health-ministry channels -- broadening, not replacing, the sectoral coordination pattern. No single longitudinal mandate has been created. Update (Jun 2026): the work-permit model preserves bounded fiscal exposure by tying camp residents' formal status to employment and a defined population (around 80,000 in nine camps, an estimated 42,000 of working age) rather than creating an open-ended entitlement; revenue effects run through payroll and consumption rather than new transfer obligations. This extends, rather than departs from, the bounded-inclusion logic already established through HICS premium/cost-recovery financing. Update (Jun 2026): the new ID-card system adds biometric registration, QR-code verification, and civil-registry integration for camp-based Myanmar Displaced Persons -- the first instance of a camp population being folded into the national civil registry rather than managed solely through parallel camp/UNHCR documentation. This is a portability-relevant development: registry inclusion is the precondition for the public-system access (banking, telecom, health) the cards are intended to unlock. It does not yet resolve fragmentation across disaster, health, welfare, and migration records more broadly, and rollout is phased by age and employment status. Infrastructure-induced displacement is less central than in Mekong dam states, but Thailand is affected by cross-border downstream impacts and return dynamics along the Myanmar border. Development finance is not yet systematically connected to displacement absorption.

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.overview: Update (Jun 2026): the paper hokhau household registration book formally ceased validity on 1 Jan 2023, replaced by an ID-number-linked national population database under the 2020 Law on Residence. Ministry of Public Security officials have stated explicitly that this is a shift in administrative form -- paper to digital -- rather than an end to residence-tied management of access and mobility. The territorial legibility this cell describes therefore persists through the new instrument rather than being resolved by it; references to 'the household registration book' as a live mechanism should be updated to the post-2023 digital system, while the underlying characterization (state-directed legibility over portability) appears to hold. Disaster and climate adaptation capacity is strong, including planned relocation in the Mekong Delta, but relocation is framed as spatial development rather than displacement protection. Health, education, poverty targeting, and social insurance are administered through territorially anchored systems. Moving across commune or registration boundaries can disrupt access regardless of underlying vulnerability. Whole-of-government coherence is strong where it serves state spatial and development objectives. The issue is not weak coordination but the orientation of coordination toward territorial control rather than longitudinal protection. Fiscal responsibility is tied to local administrative units and state development plans. Resettlement support is often project or programme based rather than embedded as portable long-term entitlement. Update (Jun 2026): the underlying data architecture for residence-based access shifted in 2023 from paper household registration books to a centralized, ID-number-linked digital population database. This could plausibly be read as a portability improvement (digital records travel more easily than paper books) or as a more efficient version of the same territorial-control logic (the database is still organized around registered residence, and de-registration/re-registration on relocation still appears to govern access). The characterization should specify which reading the evidence supports rather than describing the pre-2023 paper mechanism as if it were still current. Infrastructure and planned relocation are central. Resettlement compensation exists, but records and support do not consistently integrate with poverty or service systems after households cross administrative boundaries.
